original_abstract:
Mapping gene regulatory networks typically requires massive transcriptomic datasets to resolve connections between genes. This data requirement often stalls progress in fields where samples are scarce, such as rare diseases or conditions affecting clinically inaccessible tissues. In other domains, like natural language processing and computer vision, transfer learning has addressed similar data sparsity by leveraging deep learning models pretrained on massive, general datasets which can then be fine-tuned for specific tasks.

We developed Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based deep learning model pretrained on a large-scale corpus of approximately 30 million single-cell transcriptomes (Genecorpus-30M). This pretraining enables Geneformer to make context-specific predictions in network biology even when task-specific data is limited. During its self-supervised pretraining phase, the model acquired a fundamental understanding of network dynamics, encoding network hierarchies directly into its attention weights.

Fine-tuning Geneformer across a diverse panel of tasks—ranging from predicting chromatin dynamics to identifying network regulators—demonstrated that the model consistently improves predictive accuracy over standard methods, even with very few training examples. When applied to disease modeling with limited patient samples, Geneformer successfully identified candidate therapeutic targets for cardiomyopathy. Experimental validation showed that inhibiting these predicted targets improved the contractile function of iPSC-derived cardiac microtissues. Overall, Geneformer provides a foundational model for transfer learning in biology, offering a powerful tool to accelerate the discovery of key regulators and therapeutic targets across a broad range of human diseases.
humanized_abstract:
Inferring gene regulatory networks (GRNs) generally requires large collections of transcriptomic data to determine connections between genes. The limitation by data availability is particularly problematic in situations where sample collections are inherently limited such as in rare diseases or when the sample source is hard to access (e.g. brain tissue, etc). In many other fields, including natural language processing and computer vision, the lack of sufficient data for many tasks has been overcome by building models on large collections of public data and then applying those to the specific task at hand through a short training phase. We investigated the feasibility of applying this approach in the context of modeling gene regulatory networks and found that it could also be highly beneficial in that context.

We introduce Geneformer, a context-aware, attention-based deep learning model, pre-trained on a large-scale corpus of 30M single-cell transcriptomes (Genecorpus-30M). The Geneformer can make context-specific predictions in network biology even for limited-task data. The model is trained in a self-supervised fashion to learn prior knowledge about network dynamics in general, and embed the knowledge of network hierarchies into its attention maps.

We fine-tuned Geneformer on a wide range of biological tasks from modeling chromatin states and network regions to regulatory modules, functional variants and disease models. In all cases Geneformer outperforms state of the art benchmarks for each specific task using often as few as 5 samples for training (when available). Finally we showcase Geneformer on disease modeling with only 2 samples for each disease. We confirm the validity of its predictions by experimenting on candidate targets and show that their inhibition improves the survival and function of iPSC-derived cardiac microtissues. Geneformer serves as a foundation for future biolearning models, as it significantly shortens the time required to find key variables of interest for many types of human diseases.
